content:
# Luke Chapter 15 — Translation Disagreements

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse-by-Verse Disagreements

### 15:1 — Opening conjunction
| Translator | Rendering | Greek |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And all the tax-collectors" | ΔΕ |
| LOGOS | "Now all the tax collectors" | ΔΕ |
| PNEUMA | "Now all the tax collectors" | ΔΕ |
| **SOPHIA** | **"And all the tax collectors"** | Follows EMET — "And" preserves the narrative connective |

### 15:2 — ΔΙΕΓΟΓΓΥΖΟΝ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "murmuring" |
| LOGOS | "murmuring" |
| PNEUMA | "grumbling" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"murmuring"** | 2-to-1; preserves LXX wilderness echo |

### 15:2 — ΠΡΟΣΔΕΧΕΤΑΙ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "receives" |
| LOGOS | "receives" |
| PNEUMA | "welcomes" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"receives"** | 2-to-1; "welcomes" adds warmth beyond the accusatory context |

### 15:4 — Rhetorical question form
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "What man of you" |
| LOGOS | "What man among you" |
| PNEUMA | "Suppose one of you" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"What man among you"** | Follows LOGOS; preserves the rhetorical question form |

### 15:6 — Causal conjunction
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "because I have found" |
| LOGOS | "for I have found" |
| PNEUMA | "I have found...!" (exclamatory) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"for I have found"** | Follows LOGOS; "for" is the formal standard |

### 15:7 — "righteous ones" vs. "righteous persons"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "righteous [ones]" |
| LOGOS | "righteous persons" |
| PNEUMA | "righteous people" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"righteous ones"** | Preserves substantive adjective without adding a noun |

### 15:12 — "to the father" vs. "to his father"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "to the father" |
| LOGOS | "to his father" |
| PNEUMA | "to his father" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to his father"** | 2-to-1; more natural English |

### 15:13 — ΑΠΕΔΗΜΗΣΕΝ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "traveled-away" |
| LOGOS | "went on a journey" |
| PNEUMA | "left" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"traveled away"** | Follows EMET (without hyphen); concise and precise |

### 15:15 — ΧΟΙΡΟΥΣ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "pigs" |
| LOGOS | "swine" |
| PNEUMA | "pigs" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"pigs"** | 2-to-1; "swine" is archaic |

### 15:16 — ΕΠΕΘΥΜΕΙ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "was desiring" |
| LOGOS | "was longing" |
| PNEUMA | "longed" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"was longing"** | Follows LOGOS; "longing" captures sustained yearning |

### 15:17 — ΛΙΜΩ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "with famine" |
| LOGOS | "with hunger" |
| PNEUMA | "starving to death" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"with hunger"** | Follows LOGOS; personal experience vs. the broader famine of v. 14 |

### 15:18 — ΑΝΑΣΤΑΣ ΠΟΡΕΥΣΟΜΑΙ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Having arisen, I will go" |
| LOGOS | "I will arise and go" |
| PNEUMA | "I will get up and go back" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"I will arise and go"** | Follows LOGOS; natural English, formal register |

### 15:20 — Sequence of father's actions
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "having run, he fell upon his neck and kissed him" |
| LOGOS | "he ran and fell upon his neck and kissed him" |
| PNEUMA | "He ran to his son, threw his arms around him, and kissed him" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he ran and fell upon his neck and kissed him"** | Follows LOGOS; coordinate verbs are clearer; PNEUMA paraphrases ΕΠΕΠΕΣΕΝ ΕΠΙ ΤΟΝ ΤΡΑΧΗΛΟΝ |

### 15:22 — ΣΤΟΛΗΝ ΤΗΝ ΠΡΩΤΗΝ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the first robe" |
| LOGOS | "the first robe" |
| PNEUMA | "the best robe" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the first robe"** | 2-to-1; preserves ambiguity of ΠΡΩΤΗΝ |

### 15:23 — ΘΥΣΑΤΕ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "slaughter [it]" |
| LOGOS | "kill it" |
| PNEUMA | "kill it" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"slaughter it"** | Follows EMET; more precise for ΘΥΣΑΤΕ in animal preparation context |

### 15:24 — "this my son" vs. "this son of mine"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "this son of mine" |
| LOGOS | "this my son" |
| PNEUMA | "this son of mine" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"this my son"** | Follows LOGOS; most compact formal rendering |

### 15:28 — ΠΑΡΕΚΑΛΕΙ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "was urging him" |
| LOGOS | "was entreating him" |
| PNEUMA | "pleaded with him" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"was entreating him"** | Follows LOGOS; "entreating" captures the humble appeal |

### 15:29 — ΙΔΟΥ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold" |
| LOGOS | "Behold" |
| PNEUMA | "Look!" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Behold"** | 2-to-1; formal register preserved |

### 15:30 — ΠΟΡΝΩΝ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "prostitutes" |
| LOGOS | "harlots" |
| PNEUMA | "prostitutes" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"prostitutes"** | 2-to-1; "harlots" is archaic |

### 15:32 — ΕΔΕΙ
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "it was necessary" |
| LOGOS | "it was fitting" |
| PNEUMA | "we had to" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"it was necessary"** | Follows EMET; "necessary" preserves the full force of divine obligation; "fitting" softens it |

---

## Summary Statistics

- **Total verses:** 32
- **Full agreement (all three translators substantively identical):** 12 verses (11, 19, 21, 3, 5, 9, 10, 14, 25, 26, 31, 27 — minor wording variation only)
- **Disagreements logged:** 20
- **SOPHIA followed EMET:** 5 times
- **SOPHIA followed LOGOS:** 10 times
- **SOPHIA followed EMET+LOGOS (2-to-1):** 7 times
- **SOPHIA followed EMET+PNEUMA (2-to-1):** 3 times
- **SOPHIA independent rendering:** 1 time (15:7, "righteous ones")

### Pattern Notes

1. **PNEUMA's dynamic restructuring:** PNEUMA consistently converts Greek rhetorical questions into conditional statements (15:4, "Suppose one of you"), replaces formal conjunctions with exclamatory punctuation (15:6), and paraphrases physical actions (15:20, "threw his arms around him" for "fell upon his neck"). SOPHIA corrects toward the Greek structure in every case.

2. **Archaic vocabulary:** LOGOS retains "swine" (15:15) and "harlots" (15:30) where EMET and PNEUMA use modern equivalents. SOPHIA follows the modern rendering in both cases — formal register does not require archaism.

3. **EMET's hyphenation:** EMET continues its pattern of hyphenating compound concepts ("traveled-away," "far-off," "came-to-be," "come-to-life-again"). SOPHIA consistently removes hyphens while preserving EMET's underlying precision.

4. **15:32 — "necessary" vs. "fitting":** This is the most theologically significant disagreement in the chapter. ΕΔΕΙ expresses divine necessity (cf. Luke 2:49; 4:43; 9:22; 13:33; 24:7, 26, 44), not mere propriety. LOGOS's "fitting" weakens the father's declaration that celebration is obligatory, not optional. SOPHIA follows EMET on this point.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA.*
